parts.content: Suggest two ways in which current global patterns of food consumption are contributing to long-term resource insecurity.
parts.markscheme:
(1a) Suggest two ways in which current global patterns of food consumption are contributing to long-term resource insecurity. <marks> 2 + 2 marks </marks>

Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for a valid point and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development/example.

Answers should link food consumption to pressures on water, land, energy, or biodiversity.

Possible responses:
- Meat-heavy diets <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Livestock production requires large amounts of water, feed, and land, leading to deforestation, biodiversity loss, and water stress (e.g. Amazon deforestation for beef production) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Food waste <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Significant food loss across supply chains in HICs wastes resources like energy, fertiliser, and water used in production <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Globalised food systems <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Demand for out-of-season or exotic crops (e.g. avocados, quinoa) can strain resources in exporting countries <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Shift to ultra-processed foods <marks> 1 mark </marks>: These have higher carbon footprints and energy requirements, increasing overall environmental load <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Discuss how energy insecurity can create geopolitical tensions at both regional and global scales.
parts.markscheme:
(1b) Discuss how energy insecurity can create geopolitical tensions at both regional and global scales. <marks> 3 + 3 marks </marks>

Award up to <marks> 3 marks </marks> for each scale, with:
- <marks> 1 mark </marks> for identification
- <marks> 1 mark </marks> for explanation
- <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development/example.

Regional scale:

- Territorial disputes <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Competing claims over oil/gas reserves (e.g. South China Sea or Arctic) can cause diplomatic or military tensions <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Pipeline dependency <marks> 1 mark </marks>: States reliant on neighbours for gas/oil transit (e.g. Ukraine–Russia) are vulnerable to energy blackmail or pricing disputes <marks> 1 mark </marks>.


Global scale:
- Trade wars and sanctions <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Control of fossil fuel exports can be leveraged in international disputes (e.g. sanctions on Iran, Russia) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Strategic alliances and competition <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Countries form energy-based partnerships (e.g. China–Africa infrastructure deals), shifting global power balances <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Resource nationalism <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Countries may restrict exports, increasing tensions with import-reliant nations (e.g. rare earth elements, LNG exports) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content:
The map shows the embodied energy trade 

Source: Sankey Diagrams
![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c1sIf5hkaoINe5Kg6AaQd)



Suggest two reasons why some regions import more energy than they produce domestically.
parts.markscheme:
(2a) Suggest two reasons why some regions import more energy than they produce domestically. <marks> 2 marks </marks>

Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> per valid, clearly explained reason.

Possible responses:
- Lack of domestic energy resources <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Regions like Japan or much of the EU have limited fossil fuel reserves and must import to meet demand.
- High industrial and consumer energy demand <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Regions with strong manufacturing sectors or high per capita use (e.g. China, EU) often exceed production capacity.
- Energy mix preferences <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Some countries opt to import certain energy types (e.g. natural gas) for environmental or efficiency reasons.
- Population and economic size <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Large economies with limited local capacity (e.g. India) need to import energy at scale.

parts.content: Using specific examples from the map, examine how energy trade flows reflect patterns of global inequality in production and consumption.
parts.markscheme:
(2b) Using specific examples from the map, examine how energy trade flows reflect patterns of global inequality in production and consumption. <marks> 2 + 2 marks </marks>

Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for observation and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for interpretation, using examples.

Possible responses:
- Resource-rich regions export to industrialised economies <marks> 1 mark </marks>: The Middle East, Russia (REF), and parts of Africa export large quantities to USA, EU, and China, reflecting extraction vs. consumption disparities <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- HICs as energy consumers <marks> 1 mark </marks>: USA and EU are among the largest net importers despite technological and economic power, showing dependence on external suppliers <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Asymmetry in energy dependence <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Africa exports to multiple regions despite having lower development and energy access at home <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Global South supplying the Global North <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Embodied energy often flows from developing to developed nations via manufacturing chains (e.g. Asia to USA) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why energy-exporting regions may remain economically or politically vulnerable despite their resource advantage.
parts.markscheme:
(2c) Suggest two reasons why energy-exporting regions may remain economically or politically vulnerable despite their resource advantage. <marks> 2 + 2 marks </marks>

Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each valid point and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development/example.

Possible responses:
- Overreliance on fossil fuel exports <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Economies like Venezuela or Nigeria suffer from “resource curse” effects — corruption, volatility, and lack of diversification <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Price shocks and market dependency <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Falling oil or gas prices can destabilise revenue streams, especially in countries with weak institutions (e.g. Algeria, Iraq) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Foreign influence and exploitation <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Powerful importing nations or companies may dominate contracts or infrastructure, limiting sovereignty (e.g. oil in Chad or Angola) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Geopolitical conflict <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Resource control can attract conflict, terrorism, or foreign intervention (e.g. Iraq invasion, Niger Delta militancy) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Define the term resource security.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for a basic definition and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for further development or clarity.

- Resource security is the ability of a country or population to reliably access sufficient natural resources to meet its needs over time <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- This includes food, water, and energy, and involves both availability and access <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Describe the global pattern of food security shown on the map.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> per valid and distinct point.

Possible observations:
- Countries in North America, Europe, and Oceania generally have high food security (dark green) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Countries in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia show lower scores (orange to red) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- There is a clear pattern of higher food security in high-income countries and lower food security in low-income regions <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Latin America and Southeast Asia have mixed scores, mostly in the mid-range (yellow/green) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Explain two benefits of transitioning to renewable energy sources.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for identifying a valid benefit and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development or example per point.

Possible responses:
- Reduced greenhouse gas emissions <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Solar, wind, and hydro do not release CO₂, helping mitigate climate change <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Improved long-term energy security <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Renewables are naturally replenished and reduce reliance on imported fossil fuels <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Job creation <marks> 1 mark </marks>: The renewable sector offers new employment opportunities in solar panel installation, wind farm maintenance, etc. <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Lower air pollution <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Unlike coal and oil, renewables produce minimal local air pollutants, improving public health <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why energy demand is increasing globally.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each valid reason and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development or example.

Possible responses:
- Population growth <marks> 1 mark </marks>: More people means greater demand for electricity, transport, and heating <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Economic development <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Industrialisation and rising incomes in countries like India and China increase energy use in manufacturing and households <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Urbanisation <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Cities require more infrastructure, lighting, and transportation, all of which demand more energy <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Technology expansion <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Increased use of electronic devices, air conditioning, and electric vehicles drives up energy consumption <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why countries in Sub-Saharan Africa tend to score lower on the Global Food Security Index.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each valid reason and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for development or example.

Possible responses:
- Poverty and limited infrastructure <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Many people cannot afford sufficient or nutritious food; rural roads and cold storage are limited <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Political instability or conflict <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Disrupts food supply chains and agricultural production (e.g., in Mali, Sudan, or CAR) <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Climate-related challenges <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Droughts, floods, and changing rainfall patterns affect crop yields and livestock health <marks> 1 mark </marks>.
- Low agricultural productivity <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Limited access to modern farming methods, seeds, and irrigation reduces food output <marks> 1 mark </marks>.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which the private sector can contribute to addressing challenges in the Water-Food-Energy Nexus.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include but are not limited to:

- **Investment in Sustainable Technologies** – The private sector can drive innovation by investing in technologies that improve water efficiency, food production, and energy use, contributing to the sustainable management of all three resources.  
  - **Example: Companies like Nestlé and PepsiCo are investing in water-efficient irrigation systems for agriculture, reducing water waste in food production.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying investment in sustainable technologies as a contribution.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its impact on the water-food-energy nexus with an example.  

- **Public-Private Partnerships for Infrastructure Development** – Collaboration between private companies and governments can help build critical infrastructure that supports efficient use of water, food, and energy resources.  
  - **Example: In India, private sector involvement in developing solar-powered irrigation systems has enhanced food production while reducing reliance on traditional energy sources.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying public-private partnerships as a contribution.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how it addresses the water-food-energy nexus with an example.  

**Marking Guidance**

- **Award marks as 2+2** for two contributions from the private sector.  
- **Each response can receive up to 2 marks:**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the contribution.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its impact on the water-food-energy nexus with an example.

parts.content: Explain two reasons why understanding the Water-Food-Energy Nexus is important for sustainable resource management.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include but are not limited to:

- **Interconnectedness of Resources** – Understanding the Water-Food-Energy Nexus is crucial because the demand for one resource often impacts the others. Efficiently managing these interconnections ensures sustainable use of all resources.  
  - **Example: Increasing energy demand for irrigation can reduce available water resources, which in turn affects food production.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying interconnectedness as a reason.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its importance with an example.  

- **Informed Policy and Decision-Making** – Understanding the nexus allows policymakers to design integrated strategies that consider the needs of all sectors, leading to more effective and sustainable outcomes.  
  - **Example: In South Africa, managing water use in the energy sector is vital to ensure both energy production and food security during periods of drought.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying informed policy-making as a reason.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its importance with an example.  

**Marking Guidance**

- **Award marks as 2+2** for two reasons.  
- **Each response can receive up to 2 marks:**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the reason.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its importance with an example.

parts.content: Explain how unequal access to water and energy resources can intensify global development disparities.

- Unequal urban water access deepens social inequality <marks> 1 mark </marks>: Wealthy groups can afford private access while poorer groups depend on unreliable or polluted sources <marks> 2 marks </marks>.

parts.content: Discuss why countries with similar levels of economic development could exhibit significantly different levels of energy use per capita.

- **Energy exports and subsidies**: Resource-rich countries (e.g. Qatar, Bahrain) may use more energy due to low domestic prices and less incentive to conserve 
- **Industrial vs service economies**: Countries focused on heavy industry (e.g. China) typically use more energy than service-oriented economies (e.g. Ireland) at similar income levels 
- **Efficiency and policy differences** : Japan’s investment in energy efficiency and public transport reduces per capita use despite its economic strength

Award marks according to Geography P2 10 marker rubric.

parts.content: Describe two technological innovations that address challenges in the Water-Food-Energy Nexus.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include but are not limited to:

- **Drip Irrigation Technology** – Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the roots of plants, minimizing water waste and increasing crop yields, which is crucial in the water-food-energy nexus.  
  - **Example: In Israel, drip irrigation systems have improved agricultural water efficiency, allowing farmers to produce more food with less water.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying drip irrigation as a technological innovation.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how it addresses water and food challenges with an example.  

- **Solar-Powered Water Pumps** – Solar-powered pumps use renewable energy to extract groundwater for irrigation, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and improving water access in areas with limited electricity.  
  - **Example: In India, solar-powered pumps are used in rural areas to irrigate crops, reducing energy costs and providing a sustainable water supply for farming.**  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying solar-powered water pumps as a technological innovation.  
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how it addresses energy and water challenges with an example.

parts.content: To what extent do you agree that climate change adaptation strategies in agriculture can improve both water and energy security? Use examples from contrasting global regions.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include but are not limited to:

- **Water Efficiency in Irrigation Systems** – Adaptation strategies such as the implementation of drip irrigation and efficient water management can reduce water wastage, ensuring sustainable water use in agriculture. These strategies help address water scarcity and improve crop production, while also reducing energy use by minimizing water pumping requirements.  
  - **Example: In Israel, the use of advanced drip irrigation technologies has reduced water consumption in agriculture while boosting crop yields. This has helped address water scarcity and lowered energy use for water pumping.**  
  - **Example: In California, USA, farmers are investing in water-efficient irrigation systems to conserve water during droughts, reducing pressure on water resources and energy use for pumping.**  

- **Switch to Drought-Resistant Crops** – Adaptation strategies that involve cultivating drought-resistant crops can reduce the need for irrigation, saving water and energy. These crops are better suited to fluctuating climate conditions, ensuring that agricultural productivity is maintained with less water and energy input.  
  - **Example: In sub-Saharan Africa, farmers are increasingly using drought-tolerant maize and millet varieties to withstand dry conditions, reducing their reliance on irrigation systems and minimizing the energy needed to pump water.**  
  - **Example: In India, farmers in Rajasthan are adopting climate-resilient varieties of wheat that require less water, thereby decreasing water and energy consumption in irrigation systems.**  

- **Integration of Renewable Energy for Irrigation** – The use of solar-powered irrigation systems can help improve water security by providing an energy-efficient and sustainable way to irrigate crops, particularly in regions with limited access to electricity.  
  - **Example: In Kenya, the use of solar-powered water pumps for irrigation has reduced farmers’ dependence on grid electricity and diesel pumps, thus conserving water and energy.**  
  - **Example: In India, solar irrigation pumps are being adopted to ensure water supply to remote agricultural areas, reducing energy costs and improving water use efficiency.**  

**Marking guidance** 

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks>
  - Basic understanding of climate change impacts on agriculture
  - Limited mention of adaptation strategies
  - Minimal or no discussion of impacts on water and energy security

- <marks>3-4 marks</marks>
  - Clear explanation of climate change impacts on agriculture
  - Description of some adaptation strategies
  - Some attempt to link strategies to water or energy security
  - Limited use of examples

- <marks>5-6 marks</marks>
  - Detailed explanation of climate change impacts on agriculture
  - Description of multiple adaptation strategies
  - Clear links between strategies and both water and energy security
  - Use of at least one example from a global region

- <marks>7-8 marks</marks>
  - Comprehensive discussion of climate change impacts and adaptation strategies
  - Well-explained links between strategies and both water and energy security
  - Use of examples from at least two contrasting global regions
  - Some analysis of the interconnected nature of water, food, and energy security

- <marks>9-10 marks</marks>
  - Excellent discussion of climate change impacts and a range of adaptation strategies
  - Insightful analysis of how strategies impact both water and energy security
  - Well-chosen examples from contrasting global regions, showing depth of understanding
  - Clear recognition of the complex interactions within the Water-Food-Energy Nexus
  - Balanced consideration of both positive and negative impacts on water and energy security

<note>
For full marks, expect:
- Discussion of at least 3-4 adaptation strategies (e.g., drought-resistant crops, precision irrigation, crop diversification, altered planting schedules)
- Clear explanation of how these strategies affect both water and energy security
- Specific examples from at least two contrasting global regions (e.g., sub-Saharan Africa vs. North America)
- Recognition of both positive and negative impacts on water and energy security
- Consideration of scale (local, national, global) in the impacts of adaptation strategies
</note>

<note>
If only one region is discussed in detail, award a maximum of <marks>6 marks</marks>.
</note>

parts.content: Outline one way in which a linear economic model contributes to long-term environmental degradation.
parts.markscheme:
Award [1] for identifying a valid aspect of the linear model and [1] for development or example.
Possible responses:
A linear economy follows a "take-make-dispose" model [1], which leads to excessive waste and pollution, often through unsustainable extraction and single-use products (e.g., fast fashion or plastic packaging) [1].


It relies on continual input of raw materials [1], which depletes natural resources and accelerates deforestation, soil degradation, or water contamination [1].

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why global efforts to improve water security often fail in low-income or politically unstable regions.
parts.markscheme:
Award [1] for a valid reason and [1] for development or example.
Possible responses:
Lack of infrastructure investment [1]: Inadequate funding and poor water distribution systems limit clean water access (e.g., rural Sub-Saharan Africa) [1].


Political instability or corruption [1]: Water aid or projects may be mismanaged, diverted, or abandoned during conflict or regime change [1].


Rapid population growth or urbanisation [1]: Demand increases faster than supply, outstripping the capacity of water infrastructure (e.g., Lagos, Nigeria) [1].


Climate variability [1]: Droughts, floods, or changing rainfall patterns undermine water reliability, especially where climate resilience is weak [1].

parts.content: Explain how the growing demand for rare earth elements and critical minerals might create new global inequalities or tensions.
parts.markscheme:
Award [1] for identifying a valid point and [2] for explanation with example.
Possible responses:
Resource concentration creates dependency [1]: China controls over 60% of rare earth production, creating geopolitical leverage over tech-reliant economies (e.g., U.S., EU) [2].


Environmental degradation in supplier countries [1]: Countries like DRC or Myanmar experience pollution and unsafe mining conditions, despite being major sources of cobalt or REEs [2].


Unequal economic benefit [1]: Exporting countries often lack value-added industries and remain trapped in low-value extractive roles, while importers dominate profits through tech production (e.g., phones, EVs) [2].


Tensions over supply chains [1]: Trade restrictions, tariffs, or competition for access (e.g., U.S.–China disputes) raise risks of political or economic conflict [2].
